info.title: NookOS API
info.description: The open workspace operating system for modern software teams.

post.summary:
POST /api/v1/auth/dev-login — dev/CI escape hatch. Compiled in, but
hard-refused unless AUTH_DEV_MODE=true (and never in production).
POST /api/v1/auth/local/bootstrap — claim an unclaimed instance.
post.description:
Open only while there are no users at all. After that it is closed
permanently, so the window is "between `docker compose up` and the first
sign-in" rather than something an operator has to remember to turn off.

post.summary: POST /api/v1/auth/local/login
POST /api/v1/auth/local/password — change your own password.

get.summary: Extends `/auth/providers` with what local sign-in offers right now.

post.summary: POST /api/v1/auth/local/users — an admin creates an account.
POST /api/v1/auth/logout

get.summary:
GET /api/v1/auth/providers — unauthenticated: what sign-in methods exist,
so the login screen never offers a dead button.

parameters.name: kind
parameters.description: Kind prefix filter, e.g. "session." matches session.started/exited.

post.summary:
Queue feedback. Picks up the configured workspace unless one is given, and
delivers into the named session — creating it if this is the first time.

get.summary: Where feedback is configured to go, if anywhere yet.

put.summary: Point feedback at a repo and branch.
put.description:
Separate from submitting because the target has to be changeable: it was
previously only recorded on the first send, which meant the first repo you
ever picked was the one you were stuck with.

patch.summary: Record the pull request a piece of feedback turned into.

get.summary: What this control plane can hand out, and how to ask for it.

post.summary: Trade a join token for a certificate signed by the tenant's CA.

POST /api/v1/nodes/join — unauthenticated; the join token IS the
credential. Idempotent per (tenant, name): re-joining an existing node
rotates its token instead of creating a duplicate, which keeps container
reboots predictable. Join tokens stay valid until expiry (M1 simplicity);
`used_at` records last use for audit.
POST /api/v1/nodes/join-tokens — mint a token to enroll a new machine.
Renew on the strength of the key the node already holds.
post.description:
Works whether the certificate expired five minutes or five months ago, and
whether or not the tenant's CA rotated meanwhile — which is precisely why
the response carries the whole trust bundle rather than just a certificate.

post.summary:
Ask a node to rescan its workspace roots now, instead of waiting for the
periodic sweep. Backs `nook import`.
Cut a machine off.
post.description:
Distinct from expiry on purpose: a certificate simply running out means
"renew me", while revocation means "never again". Collapsing the two would
let a compromised machine wait out its certificate and quietly come back.

post.summary:
Open an ad-hoc terminal on a machine — a shell in the node's home directory,
no workspace required. The "just give me a prompt on that box" path.

get.summary: Resolve the "Auto (best available)" node the UI leaves selected by default.

parameters.name: workspace_id
parameters.description: Prefer a node that already hosts this workspace.

delete.summary:
Remove a session record. Kills the tmux session first when it's still
alive, so "delete" never leaves an orphan running on a node.

post.summary: Type into a session, as if a human were at the keyboard.
post.description:
This is what makes a session drivable from a script: no browser, no SSH,
no tmux knowledge — the runtime on the other end (claude, hermes, bash)
sees ordinary keystrokes. `enter` is on by default because a prompt left
sitting unsubmitted is never what the caller meant.

post.summary:
Read back what a session is showing — the visible screen plus optional
scrollback. The other half of driving a session from a script: send, then
look at what happened.

Bring a dead session back: same record, same tabs, fresh tmux session.
A terminal you closed (or a runtime that exited) shouldn't strand the
session — the node's `start` is idempotent, so this just re-issues it.

The terminals inside a session — tmux windows. Listing, opening, splitting,
focusing, closing and renaming all go through here and always answer with
the resulting list.

get.summary: What this tenant trusts, what signs, and how the rotation is progressing.

post.summary: Stage a new CA: trusted immediately, signing nothing yet.
post.description:
Step one of a rotation — distribute before switching, so machines learn the
new CA on their next renewal and nothing breaks when it starts signing.

delete.summary: Drop a CA from the trust bundle. Refused while it still has live leaves.

post.summary:
Make a staged CA the signer. The previous signer becomes `retiring` —
still trusted, no longer issuing.

get.summary: Every tenant this user is a member of, with the role they hold in each.

List this user's tokens. Never the tokens themselves — the point of the
list is deciding which one to revoke.

post.summary: Mint a token for the signed-in user.
post.description:
Requires a *user* — a node token minting user tokens would be a machine
promoting itself to its owner, which is the one thing node confinement
exists to prevent.

delete.summary: Revoke one. Immediate: the next request carrying it is unauthorized.

get.summary:
Passkeys enrolled on this vault, with their wrapped secrets — the browser
needs the blob in hand before it can ask the authenticator to open it.

post.summary:
Enrol a passkey. Requires the app password, since that's what's being
wrapped — you can't hand out a key to a vault you can't open.

delete.summary: Forget a passkey. The vault is untouched — the app password still opens it.

post.summary: Note that a passkey was just used, so the settings page can show it.

Set the app password. Once only — a second attempt is a conflict, not an
overwrite, so a stray call can never orphan existing secrets.

get.summary: Has this user set an app password yet?

post.summary:
Check a password without decrypting anything — lets the UI unlock (and
hold the password for syncing) with a clear yes/no.

409.description: the workspace still has live sessions

patch.summary: Rename a workspace — the label only.
patch.description:
Deliberately does NOT touch the slug, the checkouts on disk, or the git
remote: those are the workspace's identity, and rediscovery matches on
them. So calling a clone of `acme/services` "the flaky one" costs nothing
and breaks nothing — no directory moves, no session loses its path, and
the next heartbeat won't create a duplicate workspace.

get.summary: Live git status + working-tree diff, relayed from the node.

post.summary: Stage everything and commit, on the machine that holds the checkout.
post.description:
Finishing work shouldn't require dropping into a terminal to type the two
commands you were always going to type — you just read the diff in the panel
above the button.

post.summary: Push the checkout's current branch.

Adopt a checkout's existing `.env` into the vault, sealed with the app
password.
post.description:
Importing a repo that already carries a `.env` is the common case, and
leaving that file outside the vault meant it never travelled to the user's
other machines — and never got encrypted. This is the on-ramp: read it off
disk once, seal it, and from then on it syncs like any other secret.

get.summary:
Does this workspace have a `.env` sitting in a checkout that the vault
doesn't know about? Asked right after an import, so we only interrupt
someone for their password when there's actually something to seal.

post.summary:
Unlock a passphrase-sealed secret. The passphrase is never stored; a wrong
one is reported as such rather than as a decryption error.

AddPasskeyRequest.description:
Enrolling a passkey. The wrapping happens in the browser; the server only
ever sees the sealed blob.

AttachClientMessage.description: Terminal attach socket messages (browser → control plane).

oneOf.description:
The agreed terminal grid: the PTY is sized to the LARGEST current
viewer; every viewer renders this grid (scaling its font down if its
panel is smaller), so a small window never shrinks the session for
everyone else.

data.description:
The agreed terminal grid: the PTY is sized to the LARGEST current
viewer; every viewer renders this grid (scaling its font down if its
panel is smaller), so a small window never shrinks the session for
everyone else.

AttachServerMessage.description: Terminal attach socket messages (control plane → browser).

dev_login.description: The dev/CI escape hatch is enabled (never in production).

device_authorization_endpoint.description:
Where a native client starts a device authorization.

Read from the IdP's discovery document. `None` means the provider does
not advertise one — in which case no compliant client can start the
flow, whatever else the provider supports.

device_client_id.description:
Public client id for native clients. Distinct from the control plane's
own client, which is confidential and must not ship inside an app.

local.description: Username and password held in this database.

oidc.description: An OIDC identity provider is configured.

oidc_issuer.description:
The identity provider itself, for clients that must talk to it
directly.

A desktop app cannot receive the browser redirect this control plane
registered, so it uses the device authorization grant against the IdP.
It learns where to go from here rather than from its own configuration:
the operator sets the IdP up once, on the server, and every client
follows.

Board.description:
Provider values: `local` | `jira` | `github` | `linear` | `trello`.
External boards remain authoritative; NookOS federates.

Capabilities.description:
What a node reports about itself on registration. The control plane never
inspects a machine — the node describes its own capabilities.

agent_version.description:
The agent's own version. Reported like everything else here, so
"which machines are behind?" needs no column of its own — this whole
struct is already stored as jsonb on the node.

ssh_public_key.description:
This node's SSH public key (generated locally; the private half never
leaves the machine). Add it as a deploy key to clone private repos.

background.description:
Return as soon as the node has been asked, instead of waiting for the
clone to finish. Progress arrives as activity events carrying `job_id`.

oneOf.description: Tenant git credential to clone with (private repos over SSH).

name.description: Directory name; derived from the URL when omitted.

generate.description: …or let the server generate an ed25519 keypair.

private_key.description: Paste an existing private key (OpenSSH PEM)…

agent_url.description:
Where the joining machine should point its **agent** connection.

Not always the API's address. The agent listener terminates TLS in the
control-plane process — only it can judge a client certificate against
the right tenant's CA — so it cannot sit behind the proxy that fronts
the API, and deployments routinely give it its own name. A node told
only the API address would enrol against a URL it must not use.

ca_fingerprint.description:
SHA-256 of the certificate the joining machine should expect to see,
so it can pin the server before handing over anything. `None` when the
control plane does not terminate TLS itself (dev, or TLS at the edge),
in which case there is nothing honest to pin to.

token.description: Shown exactly once; only a hash is stored.

path.description:
Pin the session to a specific checkout path (e.g. a worktree). When
omitted, the workspace's first checkout on the node is used.

CreateTerminalRequest.description:
Open an ad-hoc terminal on a machine — a shell with no workspace, running in
the node's home directory. What you reach for when you just want a prompt on
a box, not to start work on a project.

name.description: Name the session; defaults to something like "bash · <node>".

runtime.description: The runtime to run — `bash` by default, but any the node has installed.

CreateUserTokenRequest.description: Asking for a personal access token.

expires_in_days.description: Expire it after this many days. Omit for a token that doesn't expire.

name.description: What it's for ("laptop cli", "ci"). Shown in the list you revoke from.

CreateUserTokenResponse.description: The one and only time the token itself is readable.

token.description: `nook_user_…` — store it now; the server keeps only its hash.

DeleteWorkspaceRequest.description:
Deleting a workspace. Records always go; the checkouts on disk only go
when explicitly asked for (and if they stay, discovery re-adds them).

delete_files.description: Also delete the checkout directories on every online node.

DeleteWorkspaceResponse.description: What a workspace delete actually did.

checkouts_remaining.description:
Checkouts left behind (node offline, or removal failed) — these will
be rediscovered.

DiscoveredWorkspace.description:
A git repository found under a node's workspace roots. Repositories are
self-describing; the node reports, the control plane reconciles.

worktree.description:
True when this checkout is a linked git worktree (its `.git` is a file
pointing at the primary repo, not a directory).

DispatchSuggestion.description:
The dispatcher recommends; humans approve. It never codes, edits, or
deploys — suggestions are the entire output surface.

EnrollRequest.description:
First contact: trade a join token for a certificate.

The node generates its keypair locally and sends only a CSR — the private
key never leaves the machine, so the control plane cannot leak what it was
never given.

name.description: Name for a machine enrolling for the first time.

token.description: `nook_join_…`, which is what decides whose CA signs this.

EnrollResponse.description: A certificate plus the trust the node needs to verify its peer.

ca_bundle.description:
EVERY CA this tenant trusts, not just the signer. A node that refreshed
only its own certificate would stay pinned to a CA being retired, which
is what turns a rotation into an outage.

Event.description:
Everything produces events. Kind is an open dotted string:
"node.connected", "session.started", "task.moved", "user.login", ...

next_cursor.description: Pass as `before` to fetch the next (older) page.

FeedbackItem.description: One improvement someone asked for, and what became of it.

FeedbackTarget.description: Where feedback goes — the first-run question this answers is "which repo?"

branch.description:
Branch the agent is told to work on, so improvements land somewhere
isolated and deployable rather than on whatever was checked out.

instructions.description:
What the agent should do with the change once it works — reviewed,
pushed, PR'd, left uncommitted. Overrides the built-in wording.

instructions_from_repo.description:
True when the instructions came from `.nook-feedback.md` in the repo
rather than from this setting, so the UI can say where they live.

GitCommitRequest.description: Commit everything in a checkout, from the git panel.

node_id.description: Which machine's checkout — a workspace can exist on several.

GitCredential.description: A tenant git credential — only the public half is ever returned.

oneOf.description: Tenant git credential to push with. Omit to use the node's own key.

diff.description: Unified diff of the working tree (truncated by the node if huge).

ImportSecretRequest.description: Adopt a file that already exists in a checkout into the vault.

passphrase.description:
The app password. Same rule as saving: nothing enters the vault
unsealed.

JoinRequest.description: Sent by `nook join` (unauthenticated; the join token IS the credential).

node_token.description: Long-lived node credential; shown once, stored hashed.

available.description: Local sign-in is possible: the tenant is undecided, or already local.

mode.description: "oidc" | "local" | null when nobody has signed in yet.

needs_bootstrap.description: No account exists yet, so the first visitor can claim this instance.

MeResponse.description: The signed-in caller with their tenant.

resources.description: Latest heartbeat resource sample (see `NodeResources`); `{}` until first.

NodeArtifact.description:
A node binary this control plane can hand out. One per platform it was
built with — the fleet stays on one version because the server only offers
the build it shipped with.

label.description: Human label for the picker ("macOS · Apple silicon").

url.description:
Where to download it — a GitHub release asset. The control plane no
longer hosts binaries, so it deliberately reports neither size nor
checksum: it cannot attest to bytes it does not serve, and a stale
digest is worse than none.

NodeReleases.description:
Everything the "add node" flow needs: what to download, and where the
one-shot installer lives.

base_url.description: This instance as the caller reached it — what a new machine should use.

install_url.description: URL of the generated install script.

version.description: Version of the control plane, which is the version of these binaries.

NodeResources.description:
Live resource sample a node reports on each heartbeat, so both humans and
the triage scheduler can see which machine can take the workload.

active_sessions.description: NookOS-managed sessions currently alive on the node.

load_avg1.description: 1-minute load average (0 on platforms without it).

NodeWorkspace.description:
A workspace checked out at a path on a particular node — the join table
that lets one workspace exist on many machines.

OpResponse.description: Outcome of a long-running git operation on a node.

ephemeral.description: Wipe the synced file from checkouts when the session ends.

passphrase.description:
The app password. Required, not optional: a `.env` moves between
machines, so it is sealed with something the server never stores
before the app key wraps it. A database dump plus `SECRETS_KEY` must
never be enough to read one.

RenameWorkspaceRequest.description:
A new label for a workspace. The name is what people read; the slug, the
checkouts on disk and the git remote are its identity, and none of them
move — which is what makes this safe to do on a whim.

RenewRequest.description:
Renewal: a node asks for a fresh certificate using the key it already has.

Deliberately no join token. Tokens are for a machine with no key yet;
requiring one at renewal would mean expiry costs a manual re-join, which is
exactly what must never happen to a laptop that was closed for a month.

ScheduledNode.description: The node the resource-aware scheduler chose for "Auto" placement.

SecretOnDisk.description: Whether an import left a `.env` on disk that the vault hasn't adopted yet.

checkout_path.description: Which checkout it was found in.

in_vault.description: Already stored in the vault, so there's nothing to adopt.

Session.description:
Status values: `starting` | `running` | `detached` | `exited` | `error`.
Runtime is an open string: "claude", "hermes", "codex", "bash", "zsh", ...

error.description: Why the session failed to start, when it did.

oneOf.description:
The workspace this session runs in, or `None` for an ad-hoc terminal —
a plain shell opened on a machine with no project behind it, running in
the node's home directory.

SessionInputRequest.description:
Keystrokes for a session. What a script sends instead of attaching a
terminal.

enter.description:
Press Enter afterwards. Defaults to true: an unsubmitted prompt is
almost never what a caller wanted.

SessionOutputRequest.description: How much of a session's screen to read back.

history_lines.description: Scrollback lines above the visible screen (0–2000). Default 0.

SessionOutputResponse.description:
A session's current screen, with enough context to know what you're
looking at — `runtime` is how a caller tells a claude shell from a bash one.

SessionWindow.description: One terminal inside a session (a tmux window).

panes.description: Panes in this window — >1 means it's split.

SetFeedbackTargetRequest.description:
Point feedback at a repo and a branch. Separate from submitting, so the
target can be changed at any time rather than only on the first send.

branch.description: Empty means "leave the agent to pick"; a name pins every change to it.

instructions.description:
Empty falls back to `.nook-feedback.md` in the repo, then to the
built-in wording.

SetVaultPassphraseRequest.description: Setting or checking the app password.

runtime.description: Runtime for the feedback session (defaults to claude).

oneOf.description:
Where this feedback should be worked on. Remembered for next time;
required only until one has been chosen.
Node the triage scheduler chose (or you forced) to run this work.

TenantCaSummary.description:
A tenant CA as an admin sees it. Never the private key — it is not
exportable, by design.

nodes_holding_leaves.description:
Machines still holding an unexpired leaf from this CA — the number that
says whether it can be retired yet.

TenantMembership.description:
A tenant the caller belongs to, and the role they hold in it.

Membership is deliberately its own concept: a user has one *current*
tenant (`users.tenant_id`) and may reach several, which is what teams will
be — not a new mechanism, just more rows.

current.description: The tenant this session is scoped to right now.

oneOf.description: NULL = built-in theme shipped with NookOS.

ThemeTokens.description:
Design tokens applied as CSS custom properties. Every visual aspect is
configurable; unknown keys pass through untouched so theme packs can extend.

UiEvent.description: Live events pushed to browsers over `/api/v1/ws/ui`.

UpdateSessionRequest.description: Renaming a session (tabs are named things people recognize).

User.description: Role values: `owner` | `admin` | `member` (TEXT CHECK in the schema).

UserToken.description: A personal access token as listed back — everything except the secret.

VaultPasskey.description: A passkey enrolled to unlock the vault.

credential_id.description:
Base64url WebAuthn credential id, so the browser can ask for this
specific passkey.

wrapped_secret.description:
The app password sealed under the passkey-derived key, base64. Only
the browser that holds the passkey can open it.

VaultStatus.description: Whether this user has an app password (the key that seals their secrets).

passkeys.description:
How many passkeys can unlock this vault. Non-zero means the UI should
reach for a passkey before asking anyone to type a password.

oneOf.description: Open another terminal in the session and focus it.
Split the visible terminal so two are on screen at once.

WindowAction.description: What to do with a session's terminals (tmux windows/panes).

WorkspaceLocation.description: A workspace location as presented to the UI (node join included).

worktree.description: This checkout is a linked git worktree of the workspace's primary repo.

WorkspaceSecret.description: A workspace secret file (e.g. .env). Content only present on single-get.

ephemeral.description: Removed from checkouts when the session ends.

protected.description: Sealed with a passphrase — reading it needs that passphrase.
